A diagram showing a Wi-Fi network

A **hotspot** is a physical location where people can obtain [Internet access](/wiki/Internet_access "Internet access"), typically using [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") technology, via a [wireless local-area network](/wiki/Wireless_LAN "Wireless LAN") (WLAN) using a [router](/wiki/Router_(computing) "Router (computing)") connected to an [Internet service provider](/wiki/Internet_service_provider "Internet service provider").

Public hotspots may be created by a business for use by customers, such as [coffee shops](/wiki/Coffeehouse "Coffeehouse") or hotels. Public hotspots are typically created from [wireless access points](/wiki/Wireless_access_point "Wireless access point") configured to provide Internet access, controlled to some degree by the venue. In its simplest form, venues that have [broadband Internet access](/wiki/Internet_access "Internet access") can create public wireless access by configuring an access point (AP), in conjunction with a [router](/wiki/Router_(computing) "Router (computing)") to connect the AP to the Internet. A single wireless router combining these functions may suffice.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

A private hotspot, often called [tethering](/wiki/Tethering "Tethering"), may be configured on a smartphone or tablet that has a [network](/wiki/Telecommunications_network "Telecommunications network") data plan, to allow Internet access to other devices via [password](/wiki/Password "Password"), [Bluetooth pairing](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth"), or through the [moeex](/w/index.php?title=Moeex&action=edit&redlink=1 "Moeex (page does not exist)") protocol over [USB](/wiki/USB "USB"), or even when both the hotspot device and the device[s] accessing it are connected to the same Wi-Fi network but one which does not provide Internet access. Similarly, a Bluetooth connection or [USB OTG](/wiki/USB_On-The-Go "USB On-The-Go") can be used by a mobile device to provide Internet access via Wi-Fi instead of a mobile network, to a device that itself has neither Wi-Fi nor mobile network capability passwords.

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Uses")]

The public can use a [laptop](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") or other suitable portable device to access the wireless connection (usually [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi")) provided. The iPass 2014 interactive map, that shows data provided by the analysts Maravedis Rethink, shows that in December 2014 there are 46,000,000 hotspots worldwide and more than 22,000,000 roamable hotspots. More than 10,900 hotspots are on trains, planes and airports (Wi-Fi in motion) and more than 8,500,000 are "branded" hotspots (retail, cafés, hotels). The region with the largest number of public hotspots is Europe, followed by North America and Asia.[[2]](#cite_note-ipas-map-2)

Libraries throughout the United States are implementing hotspot lending programs to extend access to online library services to users at home who cannot afford in-home Internet access or do not have access to Internet infrastructure. The [New York Public Library](/wiki/New_York_Public_Library "New York Public Library") was the largest program, lending out 10,000 devices to library patrons.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Similar programs have existed in Kansas,[[4]](#cite_note-4) Maine,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and Oklahoma;[[6]](#cite_note-6) and many individual libraries are implementing these programs.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Wi-Fi positioning](/wiki/Wi-Fi_positioning_system "Wi-Fi positioning system") is a method for [geolocation](/wiki/Geolocation "Geolocation") based on the positions of nearby hotspots.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)

## Security issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Security issues")]

Security is a serious concern in connection with public and private hotspots. There are three possible attack scenarios. First, there is the wireless connection between the client and the access point, which needs to be [encrypted](/wiki/Encrypted "Encrypted"), so that the connection cannot be eavesdropped or attacked by a [man-in-the-middle attack](/wiki/Man-in-the-middle_attack "Man-in-the-middle attack"). Second, there is the hotspot itself. The WLAN encryption ends at the interface, then travels its network stack unencrypted and then, third, travels over the wired connection up to the [BRAS](/wiki/Broadband_remote_access_server "Broadband remote access server") of the ISP.

Depending upon the setup of a public hotspot, the provider of the hotspot has access to the metadata and content accessed by users of the hotspot. The safest method when accessing the Internet over a hotspot, with unknown security measures, is [end-to-end encryption](/wiki/End-to-end_encryption "End-to-end encryption"). Examples of strong end-to-end encryption are [HTTPS](/wiki/HTTP_Secure "HTTP Secure") and [SSH](/wiki/Secure_Shell "Secure Shell").

Some hotspots [authenticate](/wiki/Authenticate "Authenticate") users; however, this does not prevent users from viewing network traffic using [packet sniffers](/wiki/Packet_sniffer "Packet sniffer").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Some vendors provide a download option that deploys [WPA](/wiki/WPA-PSK "WPA-PSK") support. This conflicts with enterprise configurations that have solutions specific to their internal [WLAN](/wiki/WLAN "WLAN").

The [Opportunistic Wireless Encryption](/wiki/Opportunistic_Wireless_Encryption "Opportunistic Wireless Encryption") (OWE) standard provides [encrypted communication](/wiki/Encrypted_communication "Encrypted communication") in open Wi-Fi networks, alongside the [WPA3](/wiki/WPA3 "WPA3") standard,[[10]](#cite_note-10) but is not yet widely implemented.

## Unintended consequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Unintended consequences")]

Main article: [LinkNYC § Nuisance complaints](/wiki/LinkNYC#Nuisance_complaints "LinkNYC")

New York City introduced a Wi-Fi hotspot kiosk called [LinkNYC](/wiki/LinkNYC "LinkNYC") with the intentions of providing modern technology for the masses as a replacement to a payphone.[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11) Businesses complained they were a homeless magnet and CBS news observed transients with wires connected to the kiosk lingering for an extended period.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It was shut down following complaints about transient activity around the station and encampments forming around it.[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11) [Transients](/wiki/Homeless "Homeless")/[panhandlers](/wiki/Panhandler "Panhandler") were the most frequent users of the kiosk since its installation in early 2016 spurring complaints about public viewing of pornography and masturbation.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Locations")]

Public hotspots are often found at [airports](/wiki/Airport "Airport"), [bookstores](/wiki/Bookselling "Bookselling"), coffee shops, [department stores](/wiki/Department_store "Department store"), [fuel stations](/wiki/Filling_station "Filling station"), [hotels](/wiki/Hotel "Hotel"), [hospitals](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital"), [libraries](/wiki/Library "Library"), public [pay phones](/wiki/Pay_phone "Pay phone"), [restaurants](/wiki/Restaurant "Restaurant"), [RV parks](/wiki/RV_park "RV park") and campgrounds, [supermarkets](/wiki/Supermarket "Supermarket"), [train stations](/wiki/Train_station "Train station"), and other public places. Additionally, many [schools](/wiki/School "School") and [universities](/wiki/University "University") have [wireless networks](/wiki/Wireless_network "Wireless network") on their campuses.

### Free hotspots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Free hotspots")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/CHE_%E2%80%94_ZH_%E2%80%94_Z%C3%BCrich_%28public_WiFi_Hotspot%29_Mattes_2022-07-29.jpg/250px-CHE_%E2%80%94_ZH_%E2%80%94_Z%C3%BCrich_%28public_WiFi_Hotspot%29_Mattes_2022-07-29.jpg)](/wiki/File:CHE_%E2%80%94_ZH_%E2%80%94_Z%C3%BCrich_(public_WiFi_Hotspot)_Mattes_2022-07-29.jpg)

Public Wi-Fi hotspot in [Zürich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich")

According to statista.com, in the year 2022, there are approximately 550 million free Wi-Fi hotspots around the world.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
The [U.S. NSA](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency") warns against connecting to free public Wi-Fi.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Free hotspots operate in two ways:

* Using an open public network is the easiest way to create a free hotspot. All that is needed is a Wi-Fi router. Similarly, when users of private wireless routers turn off their authentication requirements, opening their connection, intentionally or not, they permit [piggybacking](/wiki/Piggybacking_(Internet_access) "Piggybacking (Internet access)") (sharing) by anyone in range.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* Closed public networks use a HotSpot Management System to control access to hotspots. This software runs on the router itself or an external computer allowing operators to authorize only specific users to access the Internet.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Providers of such hotspots often associate the free access with a menu, membership, or purchase limit. Operators may also limit each user's available [bandwidth](/wiki/Bandwidth_(computing) "Bandwidth (computing)") (upload and download speed) to ensure that everyone gets a good quality service. Often this is done through [service-level agreements](/wiki/Service-level_agreement "Service-level agreement").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Commercial hotspots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Commercial hotspots")]

A commercial hotspot may feature:

* A [captive portal](/wiki/Captive_portal "Captive portal") / login screen / splash page that users are redirected to for [authentication](/wiki/Authentication "Authentication") and/or [payment](/wiki/Payment "Payment"). The captive portal / splash page sometimes includes the [social login](/wiki/Social_login "Social login") buttons.
* A payment option using a [credit card](/wiki/Credit_card "Credit card"), [iPass](/wiki/IPass_Inc. "IPass Inc."), [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal"), or another payment service (voucher-based Wi-Fi)
* A [walled garden](/wiki/Walled_garden_(technology) "Walled garden (technology)") feature that allows free access to certain sites
* [Service-oriented provisioning](/wiki/Service-oriented_provisioning "Service-oriented provisioning") to allow for improved revenue
* Data analytics and data capture tools, to analyze and export data from Wi-Fi clients

Many services provide payment services to hotspot providers, for a monthly fee or commission from the end-user income. For example, [Amazingports](/wiki/Amazingports "Amazingports") can be used to set up hotspots that intend to offer both fee-based and free internet access, and [ZoneCD](/w/index.php?title=ZoneCD&action=edit&redlink=1 "ZoneCD (page does not exist)") is a [Linux distribution](/wiki/Linux_distribution "Linux distribution") that provides payment services for hotspot providers who wish to deploy their own service.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Roaming](/wiki/Roaming "Roaming") services are expanding among major hotspot [service providers](/wiki/Service_provider "Service provider"). With roaming service the users of a commercial provider can have access to other providers' hotspots, either free of charge or for extra fees, which users will usually be charged on an access-per-minute basis.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Software hotspots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Software hotspots")]

Main article: [SoftAP](/wiki/SoftAP "SoftAP")

Many Wi-Fi adapters built into or easily added to consumer computers and mobile devices include the functionality to operate as private or mobile hotspots, sometimes referred to as "mi-fi".[[18]](#cite_note-18) The use of a private hotspot to enable other personal devices to access the [WAN](/wiki/Wide_area_network "Wide area network") (usually but not always the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet")) is a form of [bridging](/wiki/Bridging_(networking) "Bridging (networking)"), and known as tethering. Manufacturers and [firmware](/wiki/Custom_rom "Custom rom") creators can enable this functionality in Wi-Fi devices on many Wi-Fi devices, depending upon the capabilities of the hardware, and most modern consumer operating systems, including [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"), [Apple OS X](/wiki/Apple_OS_X "Apple OS X") 10.6 and later,[[19]](#cite_note-19) [Windows](/wiki/Windows "Windows"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) and [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] include features to support this. Additionally wireless chipset manufacturers such as [Atheros](/wiki/Atheros "Atheros"), [Broadcom](/wiki/Broadcom "Broadcom"), [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") and others, may add the capability for certain Wi-Fi [NICs](/wiki/Network_interface_controller "Network interface controller"), usually used in a client role, to also be used for hotspot purposes. However, some service providers, such as AT&T,[[21]](#cite_note-21) Sprint,[[22]](#cite_note-22) and T-Mobile[[23]](#cite_note-23) charge users for this service or prohibit and disconnect user connections if tethering is detected.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Third-party software vendors offer applications to allow users to operate their own hotspot, whether to access the Internet when on the go, share an existing connection, or extend the range of another hotspot.

### Hotspot 2.0

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Hotspot 2.0")]

Hotspot 2.0, also known as HS2 and Wi-Fi Certified Passpoint,[[24]](#cite_note-24) is an approach to public access Wi-Fi by the [Wi-Fi Alliance](/wiki/Wi-Fi_Alliance "Wi-Fi Alliance"). The idea is for mobile devices to automatically join a Wi-Fi subscriber service whenever the user enters a Hotspot 2.0 area, in order to provide better bandwidth and services-on-demand to end-users and relieve carrier infrastructure of some traffic.

Hotspot 2.0 is based on the [IEEE 802.11u](/wiki/IEEE_802.11u "IEEE 802.11u") standard, which is a set of protocols published in 2011 to enable cellular-like roaming. If the device supports 802.11u and is subscribed to a Hotspot 2.0 service it will automatically connect and roam.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

#### Supported devices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Supported devices")]

* Apple mobile devices running [iOS 7](/wiki/IOS_7 "IOS 7") and up[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* Some [Samsung Galaxy](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy "Samsung Galaxy") smartphones[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* [Windows 10](/wiki/Windows_10 "Windows 10") devices have full support for network discovery and connection.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30)
* Windows 8 and Windows 8.1 lack network discovery, but support connecting to a network when the credentials are known.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30)

## Billing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Billing")]

EDCF user-priority list

|  | | Net traffic | | | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| low | | | high | | |
| Audio | Video | Data | Audio | Video | Data |
| User needs | time-critical | 7 | 5 | 0 | 6 | 4 | 0 |
| not time-critical | - | - | 2 | - | - | 2 |

The "user-fairness model" is a dynamic billing model, which allows volume-based billing, charged only by the amount of payload (data, video, audio). Moreover, the tariff is classified by net traffic and user needs.[[31]](#cite_note-pommer-31)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

If the net traffic increases, then the user has to pay the next higher tariff class. The user can be prompted to confirm that they want to continue the session in the higher traffic class.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Wi-Fi_hotspot#Dubious "Talk:Wi-Fi hotspot")*] A higher class fare can also be charged for delay sensitive applications such as video and audio, versus non time-critical applications such as reading Web pages and sending e-mail.

Tariff classes of the user-fairness model

|  | | Net traffic | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| low | high |
| User needs | time-critical | standard | exclusive |
| not time-critical | low priced | standard |

The "User-fairness model" can be implemented with the help of EDCF (IEEE 802.11e). An EDCF user priority list shares the traffic in 3 access categories (data, video, audio) and user priorities (UP).[[31]](#cite_note-pommer-31)

* Data [UP 0|2]
* Video [UP 5|4]
* Audio [UP 7|6]

See [Service-oriented provisioning](/wiki/Service-oriented_provisioning "Service-oriented provisioning") for viable implementations.

## Legal issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Legal issues")]

See also: [Legality of piggybacking](/wiki/Legality_of_piggybacking "Legality of piggybacking")

Depending upon the set up of a public hotspot, the provider of the hotspot has access to the metadata and content accessed by users of the hotspot, and may have legal obligations related to privacy requirements and liability for use of the hotspot for unlawful purposes.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In countries where the internet is regulated or [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") more restricted, there may be requirements such as licensing, logging, or recording of user information.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Concerns may also relate to [child safety](/wiki/Child_safety "Child safety"), and [social issues](/wiki/Social_issue "Social issue") such as exposure to objectionable content, protection against [cyberbullying](/wiki/Cyberbullying "Cyberbullying") and illegal behaviours, and prevention of perpetration of such behaviors by hotspot users themselves.

### European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: European Union")]

The [Data Retention Directive](/wiki/Data_Retention_Directive "Data Retention Directive") which required hotspot owners to retain key user statistics for 12 months was annulled by the Court of Justice of the European Union in 2014. The [Directive on Privacy and Electronic Communications](/wiki/Directive_on_Privacy_and_Electronic_Communications "Directive on Privacy and Electronic Communications") was replaced in 2018 by the [General Data Protection Regulation](/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation "General Data Protection Regulation"), which imposes restrictions on data collection by hotspot operators.

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

* [Data Protection Act 1998](/wiki/Data_Protection_Act_1998 "Data Protection Act 1998"): The hotspot owner must retain individual's information [*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Wi-Fi_hotspot#Dubious "Talk:Wi-Fi hotspot")*] within the confines of the law.
* [Digital Economy Act 2010](/wiki/Digital_Economy_Act_2010 "Digital Economy Act 2010") :[[39]](https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/sn05515/#:~:text=Download%20full%20report-,The%20Digital%20Economy%20Act%202010%20received%20Royal%20Assent%20at%20the,history%20provided%20would%20be%20anonymised.) Deals with, among other things, [copyright infringement](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement"), and imposes fines of up to £250,000 for contravention.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wi-Fi_hotspot&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/CDSC_wifi_Classon_jeh.jpg/250px-CDSC_wifi_Classon_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:CDSC_wifi_Classon_jeh.jpg)

Public park in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") has free Wi-Fi from a local corporation.

Public access wireless [local area networks](/wiki/Local_area_network "Local area network") (LANs) were first proposed by Henrik Sjoden at the NetWorld+Interop conference in The [Moscone Center](/wiki/Moscone_Center "Moscone Center") in San Francisco in August 1993.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Sjoden did not use the term "hotspot" but referred to publicly accessible wireless LANs.

The first commercial venture to attempt to create a public local area access network was a firm founded in Richardson, Texas known as PLANCOM (Public Local Area Network Communications). The founders of the venture, Mark Goode, Greg Jackson, and Brett Stewart dissolved the firm in 1998, while Goode and Jackson created [MobileStar Networks](/wiki/MobileStar "MobileStar"). The firm was one of the first to sign such public access locations as Starbucks,[[34]](#cite_note-34) American Airlines,[[35]](#cite_note-35) and Hilton Hotels.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The company was sold to Deutsche Telecom in 2001, who then converted the name of the firm into "T-Mobile Hotspot". It was then that the term "hotspot" entered the popular vernacular as a reference to a location where a publicly accessible wireless LAN is available.

[ABI Research](/wiki/ABI_Research "ABI Research") reported there was a total of 4.9 million global Wi-Fi hotspots in 2012.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In 2016 the [Wireless Broadband Alliance](/wiki/Wireless_Broadband_Alliance "Wireless Broadband Alliance") predicted a steady annual increase from 5.2m public hotspots in 2012 to 10.5m in 2018.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
